title: Building a Bonding Curve Token on Midnight with Real ZK Proofs
summary: Step-by-step implementation of a bonding curve token on Midnight using Compact. Covers off-chain computation with witnesses, on-chain ZK verification, maintaining reserve invariants, and how this model differs from traditional EVM-based token design.
author: Tushar_Pamnani_

title: Why Midnight Chooses ZKPs Over FHE for Scalable Data Protection
summary: Explains why Midnight uses zero-knowledge proofs instead of fully homomorphic encryption. Breaks down trade-offs in performance, composability, and developer usability, and why ZKPs are more practical for real-world privacy-preserving applications.
author: Meek Owen's

title: The Builder’s Guide to Tokens on the Midnight Network
summary: A practical guide to designing tokens on Midnight. Covers privacy-aware token models, selective disclosure, compliance considerations, and how Midnight’s architecture changes standard ERC-style token assumptions.
author: itsNikku876

title: Midnight Blockchain Architecture (Layman Explanation)
summary: A simplified breakdown of Midnight’s architecture, including its execution model, privacy layers, and how it combines off-chain computation with on-chain verification to enable confidential smart contracts.
author: itsNikku876

title: You're probably using exportLedger wrong
summary: Deep dive into Midnight state design. Explains when to use exportLedger vs private state, common mistakes devs make by copying EVM patterns, and how to structure contracts for intentional data exposure and privacy.
author: Tushar Pamnani

title: How Midnight Coordinates Two-Party Transfers
summary: Explains the mechanics of private two-party transfers on Midnight, including coordination between participants, witness generation, and how correctness is enforced without revealing transaction details.
author: Tushar Pamnani

title: Why This Launch Matters for Blockchain’s Next Chapter
summary: High-level perspective on Midnight’s launch and its impact on blockchain evolution. Focuses on the shift toward programmable privacy, regulatory alignment, and new categories of applications enabled by confidential computation.
author: Meek Owen's

title: Selective Disclosure: Self-Managing DIDs for AI Agents
summary: Explores decentralized identity for AI agents using selective disclosure. Covers how agents can prove attributes without exposing full identity, and how Midnight enables self-sovereign, privacy-preserving DID systems.
author: Alex Pestchanker

title: How Midnight Verifies Tokens Without Computing It
summary: Breaks down how Midnight verifies token state using proofs instead of direct computation. Covers witness-based design, verification flows, and why this model improves scalability and privacy.
author: Tushar Pamnani

title: Building Privacy-Preserving Decentralized Identity on Midnight
summary: Guide to building decentralized identity systems on Midnight. Covers credential issuance, selective disclosure, and how to design identity flows that preserve privacy while remaining verifiable.
author: Nasihudeen Jimoh

title: DUST vs NIGHT: Rethinking How Blockchains Handle Value and Fees
summary: In most blockchains, one token does everything it stores value and pays for transactions. For example, on Ethereum you use ETH for both holding value and paying gas fees, and on Bitcoin BTC serves a similar dual purpose.
author: itsNikku876

title: The Builder’s Guide to Tokens : on the Midnight Network
summary: Blockchain is powerful because it is open and transparent. But that also creates a problem. Everyone can see everything, which is not always good when dealing with sensitive data. This is called the transparency paradox.
author: itsNikku876

title: ZK Membership Proofs on Midnight
summary: Proving you belong without revealing who you are: that's the core idea behind ZK membership proofs. On Midnight, you can prove your address is on an allowlist, holds a credential, or meets a condition, without exposing the address itself or anything else about you. The proof is the only thing that goes on-chain.
author: itsNikku876
Tushar Pamnani
